title: The Memorial
desc: Kiru City goes quiet once a year. Today is the day.

steps.text:
Once a year, Kiru City goes quiet.

The markets close. The training yards empty. Even the wind seems to hold its breath. Today is the Remembering — when the OverWorld honors those who fell defending it.

You've been asked to help carry stone tablets from the archive to the central plaza. Each one bears a name. Some are ancient, worn smooth by centuries of hands touching them. Others are painfully fresh, the chisel marks still sharp.

The tablet you're carrying reads a name you don't recognize. But the OverWorlder walking beside you does. Her grip on her own tablet tightens. She doesn't cry. She just walks a little slower.

You match her pace. The procession moves through the streets in silence — dozens of creatures carrying stone tablets, each one a name, each name a life.

In the plaza, the tablets are arranged in a spiral — the oldest at the center, the newest at the edge. The spiral grows every year. No one talks about that.

A single note of Mugic rises from the crowd. Then another. Then a hundred voices join, not singing the same melody but weaving around each other, filling in each other's silences. It's not beautiful in the way music usually is. It's beautiful in the way that grief shared becomes something you can carry.

The OverWorlder beside you places her tablet at the spiral's edge. She touches the name once. Then she walks away.

"My teacher," she says eventually. "He held Cordac Falls for three days alone so the rest of the garrison could retreat. They wrote songs about it. I'd rather he was here to hear them."

You carry the tablets in silence after that. In the plaza, you help arrange them in the spiral. Her teacher's tablet goes near the outer edge — recent enough that the stone still smells of the chisel.

The ceremony begins. No speeches. No commanders. Just Mugic — voices rising and falling, weaving a harmony that seems to include the dead. The tablets vibrate faintly, resonating with the sound.

When it's over, the OverWorlder finds you. "Thank you for asking," she says. "Most people don't. Most people look at the tablet and see stone. You saw that it was a person."

She walks away. The plaza empties. The tablets stay.
